content: {"notes": [{"url": "https://earth.org/arctic-permafrost-abrupt-thaw-a-risk/", "title": "Arctic Permafrost Moving Toward Crisis, Abrupt Thaw a ...", "summary": "The cracked, buckling road between Fairbanks and Fox, Alaska, wends its way past snowy forests where slender birches and black spruce trees protrude out of the ground at impossible angles, slanting and swaying as if the very earth were alive. Scientists call these \u2018drunken forests\u2019. The ground here consists of permafrost \u2014 soil, sediment, rock and ice that often remains at or below 0 degrees Celsius year-round.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The ground here consists of permafrost \u2014 soil, sediment, rock and ice that often remains at or below 0 degrees Celsius year-round.", "Permafrost covers approximately 22.8 million square kilometres in the Arctic, sub-Arctic and alpine regions \u2014 comprising nearly a quarter of the exposed land surface in the northern hemisphere. \u2014 In Alaska, 85% of the state lies within the permafrost", "Scientists began seriously worrying about climate change impacts on permafrost about 15 years ago, but not only because of degraded infrastructure.", "An estimated 1 400 gigatons of carbon \u2014 made up of decomposed plants and animals which once inhabited the Earth \u2014 can be found embedded in permafrost.", "By comparison, our atmosphere presently contains just 850 gigatons of carbon."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The ground here consists of permafrost \u2014 soil, sediment, rock and ice that often remains at or below 0 degrees Celsius y"}, {"url": "https://research.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/53006", "title": "Long-term CO2 production following permafrost thawing", "summary": "Sigsgaard | | Year: | 2013 | | Type: | Scientific Journal | | Station: | Pacific Northwest Research Station | Abstract Thawing permafrost represents a poorly understood feedback mechanism of climate change in the Arctic, but with a potential impact owing to stored carbon being mobilized1\u20135. We have quantified the long-term loss of carbon (C) from thawing permafrost in Northeast Greenland from 1996 to 2008 by combining repeated sediment sampling to assess changes in C stock and >12 years of CO2 production in incubated permafrost samples. Field observations show that the activelayer thickness has increased by >1 cm yr-1 but thawing has not resulted in a detectable decline in C stocks.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Sigsgaard | | Year: | 2013 | | Type: | Scientific Journal | | Station: | Pacific Northwest Research Station | Abstract Thawing permafrost represents a poorly understood feedback mechanism of climate change in the Arctic, but with a potential impact o", "We have quantified the long-term loss of carbon (C) from thawing permafrost in Northeast Greenland from 1996 to 2008 by combining repeated sediment sampling to assess changes in C stock and >12 years of CO2 production in incubated permafrost samples.", "Field observations show that the activelayer thickness has increased by >1 cm yr-1 but thawing has not resulted in a detectable decline in C stocks.", "Laboratory mineralization rates at 5 C resulted in a C loss between 9 and 75%, depending on drainage, highlighting the potential of fast mobilization of permafrost C under aerobic conditions, but also that C at near-saturated conditions may remain la", "This is confirmed by a three-pool C dynamics model that projects a potential C loss between 13 and 77% for 50 years of incubation at 5 C."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Sigsgaard | | Year: | 2013 | | Type: | Scientific Journal | | Station: | Pacific Northwest Research Station | Abstract T"}, {"url": "https://climate.esa.int/en/news-events/long-term-permafrost-record-details-arctic-thaw/", "title": "Long-term permafrost record details Arctic thaw", "summary": "Frozen Arctic soils are set to release vast amounts of greenhouse gases to the atmosphere as they continue to thaw in coming decades. Despite concerns that this will fuel future global warming, the scale and speed of this important climate process remain uncertain. To help address this knowledge gap, ESA-funded researchers have developed and released a new permafrost dataset \u2013 the longest, satellite-derived permafrost record currently available.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The new 21-year satellite-derived record details the annual changes to the northern hemisphere permafrost soils from 1997\u20142018.", "Centre for Environmental Data Analysis, 02 November 2020. doi:10.5285/28e889210f884b469d7168fde4b4e54f. http://dx.doi.org/10.5285/28e889210f884b469d7168fde4b4e54f"], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The new 21-year satellite-derived record details the annual changes to the northern hemisphere permafrost soils from 199"}, {"url": "https://lamont.columbia.edu/news/permafrost-emissions-must-be-factored-global-climate-targets-says-study", "title": "Permafrost Emissions Must Be Factored Into Global Climate Targets ...", "summary": "Permafrost Emissions Must Be Factored Into Global Climate Targets, Says Study As the Arctic melts, permafrost there has the potential to send huge amounts of carbon into the atmosphere, but exactly how much is up for grabs, depending on what we do to stem climate change in coming years. By the end of this century, permafrost in the rapidly warming Arctic will likely emit as much carbon dioxide and methane into the atmosphere as a large industrial nation, and over time potentially more than the United States has emitted since the start of the Industrial Revolution. But that\u2019s only one possible future for the vast stores of carbon locked in formerly perennially frozen but now-thawing ground in the Arctic.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The team estimates that under a low warming scenario\u2014one that could be achieved if the global community limited warming to 2 degrees C or below by reducing fossil fuel emissions\u2014permafrost would release 55 billion metric tons of greenhouse gases by t", "This forms lakes or causes other changes to surface hydrology.", "Once formerly frozen ground erodes or subsides, the carbon stored there can enter the atmosphere via microbial respiration."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The team estimates that under a low warming scenario\u2014one that could be achieved if the global community limited warming "}, {"url": "https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx", "title": "[DOC] attachment_1.docx - Regulations.gov", "summary": "Woodwell Climate Research Center (\u201cWoodwell Climate\u201d) is a non-profit organization based in Falmouth, Massachusetts that comprises research scientists and policy experts who work with partners worldwide to understand the impacts of and to address climate change on a global scale. Woodwell Climate scientists helped to launch the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change in 1992 and shared the Nobel Prize awarded to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) in 2007. The organization continues to have global impact, bringing together cutting-edge science and translating scientific advances into climate policy solutions.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Woodwell Climate scientists helped to launch the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change in 1992 and shared the Nobel Prize awarded to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) in 2007.", "While several US federal agencies are actively involved in safeguarding the Arctic from environmental degradation and mitigating adverse effects of climate change,[2](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote2s"], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Woodwell Climate scientists helped to launch the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change in 1992 and share"}]}
